title: Gap probabilities and applications to geometry and random topology
speaker: Antonio Lerario
abstract: What is the volume of the set of singular symmetric matrices of norm one? What is the probability that a random plane misses this set? What is the expected "topology" of the intersection of random quadric hypersurfaces?

proof_stages.label: Real discriminant volume problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines Sigma_n as the unit-Frobenius hypersurface of singular real symmetric matrices, contrasts it with easier complex and real-versus-complex comparison principles, and states the real asymptotic volume ratio as proportional to n^{1/2}.

proof_stages.label: Tube-to-gap-probability reduction
proof_stages.summary: Using the GOE eigenvalue density, the volume of Sigma_n is written as the derivative of an epsilon-tube, identified by the Eckart-Young theorem with a least-singular-value condition, and converted from a conic GOE probability to the derivative at zero of a gap probability.

proof_stages.label: Gap-derivative asymptotics and beta variants
proof_stages.summary: The finite-n derivative at zero of the gap probability is evaluated from the joint eigenvalue density, yielding the n^{-1/2} asymptotic needed for the volume theorem and extending to beta, Hermitian, and quaternionic ensembles.

proof_stages.label: Random quadrics and square-root topology theorem
proof_stages.summary: The second problem asks for expected Betti numbers of a Kostlan/GOE random intersection of k quadrics in real projective space, and for two quadrics states a total-Betti asymptotic and a profile where the nontrivial excess is concentrated near the middle dimension.

proof_stages.label: Deterministic index-table reduction
proof_stages.summary: For fixed quadrics the argument passes to their linear span, records the maximal positive index and the topology of its intersection with the discriminant Sigma_n, and packages these data in a table whose diagonals bound or compute Betti numbers of the common zero set.

proof_stages.label: Random asymptotics of the index table
proof_stages.summary: Random-matrix estimates, including Wigner semicircle input and covering arguments, show that the normalized maximal positive index tends to 1/2 and that the discriminant contribution is squeezed near the middle rows, forcing off-middle expected Betti numbers to be essentially 1 for fixed k.

proof_stages.label: Two-quadric middle contribution from discriminant volume
proof_stages.summary: When k=2, the middle table contribution is the number of singular lines in a random GOE plane, whose expectation is given by integral geometry as the normalized volume of Sigma_n, so the earlier gap-probability volume theorem supplies the announced total-Betti asymptotic.

proof_stages.label: Three-quadric determinantal-curve problem
proof_stages.summary: For k=3 the unresolved middle contribution becomes the expected topology of a random determinantal curve on RP^2, connecting the problem to determinantal representations of real plane curves, hyperbolic curves, Vinnikov/Lax phenomena, and probabilities of empty spectrahedral sections.

examples.name: Two-by-two symmetric discriminant on the sphere
examples.note: For n=2 the real symmetric matrices form R^3, the determinant-zero cone cuts the unit sphere in two ellipses, and the volume 2√2π makes the discriminant-volume problem concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: He specializes to n=2, identifies the symmetric matrices with R^3, describes the determinant-zero cone and its intersection with S^2, and gives the resulting volume 2√2π.

examples.name: Complex degree-volume benchmark
examples.note: The complex symmetric/projective version has volume equal to degree times the corresponding projective-space volume, serving as a contrast to the subtler real case.
examples.engagement_reason: He gives the complex volume formula and sketches its derivation via integral geometry, averaging plane intersections to recover the degree n.

examples.name: Real roots, Smith inequality, and Harnack-type comparisons
examples.note: Polynomial roots and real plane curves illustrate the general pattern that real geometry and topology are bounded by complex counterparts, and later motivate square-root behavior after randomization.
examples.engagement_reason: He applies real-versus-complex comparison principles, in particular Smith inequality, to derive Harnack-type bounds for plane curves from the complex Betti/genus count.

examples.name: Hyperbolic nested real plane curve
examples.note: A maximally nested hyperbolic curve illustrates Vinnikov’s definite determinantal representation theorem and why random determinantal curves connect the k=3 middle-Betti problem to Lax-type and spectrahedral questions.
examples.engagement_reason: He cites hyperbolic maximally nested curves as an instance of Vinnikov’s determinantal representation theorem and states the positive-definite-combination property without working it out.
